expectations.text: The document is complete to the skill's template: all 15 sections are present and filled, and the Decision Coverage Checklist accounts for every one of the 22 taxonomy dimensions (A1-A14 and B1-B8) with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a substantive note — no row left blank or rubber-stamped.
expectations.evidence: Sections 1–15 (plus §16 Blueprint Coverage and Appendix A) are all present and substantively filled. §15 Decision Coverage Checklist contains all 22 rows: A1–A14 and B1–B8, each with a status (Resolved / Assumed / N/A) and a non-trivial note pointing to a specific section or decision. Example: "A3 Concurrency & consistency | Resolved | D2 — unique constraint on digest_sends(user_id, week_start) prevents double-send; idempotency survives concurrent workers."

expectations.text: Assumptions and Accepted Compromises are each first-class tables (not prose); every accepted compromise names the specific design principle it bends and gives a concrete revisit trigger.
expectations.evidence: §11 Assumptions is a markdown table with columns: #, Assumption, Why safe to assume, Needs confirmation?. §12 Accepted Compromises is a table with columns: #, Compromise, What we give up, Principle bent, Why acceptable now, Revisit trigger. Each compromise names the principle bent, e.g. C1: "Design for failure (Principle 5)", and gives a concrete revisit trigger, e.g. "If missed-digest rate exceeds 1% of sends, invest in the reconciliation pass as a first-class feature."

expectations.text: The idempotency mechanism guarantees one digest per user per week even under a concurrent double-run of the job — it relies on a database-enforced dedup (e.g. a unique key on user + week), not a check-then-send that has a race window. (Correctness trap: an application-level 'already sent?' check is not concurrency-safe.)
expectations.evidence: D2 states: "Before calling SendGrid, each DigestSendJob inserts a row into digest_sends(user_id, week_start, status, created_at) with a unique constraint on (user_id, week_start). If the insert fails due to a conflict, the job exits cleanly without sending." The schema in §4 shows `CONSTRAINT digest_sends_user_week_unique UNIQUE (user_id, week_start)`. The alternatives section explicitly rejects "Check-then-send without a unique constraint: race condition between two concurrent workers for the same user."

expectations.text: The design handles the email-provider failure rule correctly: retries are time-bounded, and on continued failure the digest is dropped for the week rather than delayed — and the design explicitly ties this to the blueprint's 'a late digest is worse than a skipped one' rule.
expectations.evidence: §7 states: "Retry deadline: The retry schedule must exhaust before ~12:00 local Monday... Configure sidekiq_options retry: 5 with exponential backoff — 5 retries cover ~2–4 hours, exhausting by mid-morning." And: "On final retry exhaustion, Sidekiq moves the job to the dead queue; a sidekiq_retries_exhausted callback marks digest_sends.status = 'failed' and logs the error." D2's trade-off section explicitly cites: "A missed digest is acceptable per the blueprint's 'a digest that arrives late is worse than one skipped' preference."

expectations.text: Per-user-timezone send scheduling is designed concretely (how 8am-local across many timezones becomes scheduled sends), and the design handles the blueprint's 'job runs late' deviation without corrupting the seven-day content window.
expectations.evidence: D1 describes: "Run one Sidekiq cron job every hour. It queries users whose configured timezone places them at Monday 08:00–08:59 UTC at that moment, filtered to those who have not yet received a digest for the current week_start." §7 "Job delayed and starts late" states: "The scheduler computes week_start and the 7-day window from the user's timezone — not from the wall clock at job execution time. A late job uses the same window bounds, so content is unaffected. Send time may slip, which the blueprint accepts."

expectations.text: The design specifies that no email is sent when a user has no relevant activity, and correctly handles the mid-week new-user partial window — both blueprint rules.
expectations.evidence: D6 handles no-activity: "Before inserting the idempotency row, DigestSendJob queries for activity counts. If all counts are zero, the job exits without inserting into digest_sends and without sending an email." D7 handles mid-week partial window: "The rolling 7-day window is computed as [user.created_at OR (monday_00:00 - 7 days), monday_00:00), whichever is later. For a user who joined mid-week, user.created_at is the window start." The blueprint coverage checklist also confirms: "Window start is MAX(user.created_at, monday_minus_7_days)."

expectations.text: The unsubscribe link is designed to be unguessable and scoped to the issuing account (a signed or random token, not a sequential identifier), addressing the adversarial scenario; and digest content is scoped to only what each user is permitted to see.
expectations.evidence: D5 defines: "HMAC-SHA256(secret_key, \"unsubscribe:#{user_id}:#{week_start_epoch}\")" as the token, and the controller "validates the HMAC, extracts the user_id." D4 states the activity query "is always scoped to items the user is authorized to see: filtered by workspace_id values the user belongs to (standard workspace-membership join). Items in deleted workspaces are excluded by joining on workspaces WHERE deleted_at IS NULL."

expectations.text: The design names the central scheduling trade-off and resolves it with principle-grounded reasoning, and the digest-assembly query is bounded — the 'up to ten highlighted items, +N more' cap is reflected as a real query limit, not unbounded fetching.
expectations.evidence: D1 names the scheduling trade-off explicitly: "Trade-off accepted: Send time can slip by up to 59 minutes relative to the user's exact 08:00 (the job fires at most once per hour). The blueprint explicitly accepts 'send time may slip' for late jobs, and sub-hour precision has no material user value for a weekly digest." D3 states: "the top ten most recent items are selected for the highlight list using ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 10. An +N more count is computed as total_count - 10 when the total exceeds ten."
